planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Set out cutting-grown starts after frost in full sun with well-drained soil. Large clusters of deep cobalt-blue flowers cover a vigorous, spreading, mildew-resistant plant. Water at the base, feed regularly, and trim after each flush to keep the plant full and blooming into fall.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Grown from cuttings, plant starts after frost in full sun and well-drained soil. Each flower shows a bold magenta-and-white star pattern that gives the clusters a striped, pinwheel look. Water at the base, feed regularly, and clip off faded heads to keep the patterned blooms coming.

planting.note: Sow once nights stay warm and the soil has warmed well past frost.

directions: Press seeds about a quarter inch into warm, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. This bicolor bears scarlet-and-gold blooms that shift as they age, so pinch young plants once to branch them. Deadhead or cut regularly to keep the show coming until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost when soil is warm.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in loose, fertile ground and thin seedlings to a hand's width apart for airflow. The large double flowers carry contrasting bicolor tips, giving a pinwheel effect prized for cutting. Harvest stems in the cool morning just as blooms fully open.

planting.note: Wait for warm soil after frost danger passes before sowing.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in a sunny, warm bed and keep the surface damp through germination. This heirloom produces large, dahlia-style blooms in a rich scarlet-red on tall, sturdy stems. Cut freely for bouquets, since removing flowers keeps the plant branching and blooming.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil has warmed after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep moist until it sprouts, then let this tough interspecific hybrid dry between waterings. Compact, mounding plants shrug off heat, humidity, and mildew while covering themselves in single red blooms. No deadheading is needed since spent flowers drop cleanly and rebloom continues to frost.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly in a sunny spot and thin to about ten inches so the mounds knit into a low carpet. Bright single yellow daisies bloom nonstop on disease-resistant foliage that tolerates drought once established. Plants are self-cleaning, so simply enjoy the color from early summer until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil warms, or start indoors a few weeks earlier.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in rich, warm soil and keep lightly moist until they emerge. Expect a mix of scabiosa-type pompon flowers with raised centers in dusky rose-purple tones, along with some flatter singles. Cut stems as the crested centers form for long-lasting, distinctive bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after all danger of frost.

directions: Scatter seed and barely cover it in a hot, sunny bed where drainage is sharp. Wiry, sprawling stems carry small starry red flowers with curled, quilled petals that flutter in the breeze. Very heat- and drought-tolerant, it needs little care beyond occasional watering during long dry spells.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost once soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm ground and thin to about eight inches apart. This Mexican zinnia bears small mahogany-red flowers edged and tipped in golden orange on bushy, heat-loving plants. Deadhead lightly to prolong the bicolor display through the hottest months.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant seeds shallowly in full sun and keep the bed moist only until germination. Single, daisy-like flowers open deep mahogany-red with broad golden-yellow tips, dependable in heat and drought. Cut or deadhead often to encourage a steady flush of new bicolor blooms.

planting.note: Sow directly once nights are warm and frost is past.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm, sunny soil and thin to give each plant room to bush out. The small semi-double flowers come in a lively blend of red, orange, and gold, often with contrasting tips. These tough little zinnias thrive in heat and reward regular picking with more blooms.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed in a sunny, well-drained bed and water sparingly once plants are up. This narrowleaf zinnia forms low, spreading mounds smothered in small white daisies with gold centers. Highly mildew-resistant and self-cleaning, it blooms tirelessly through heat until frost with no deadheading.

planting.note: Sow after frost when the soil is warm to the touch.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in fertile, warm soil and keep evenly moist until sprouting. This heirloom produces soft primrose-to-pale-yellow double flowers that fade toward cream, prized for gentle bouquet tones. Pinch young plants and cut often to keep long stems and continuous bloom coming.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant out after frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm soil and thin seedlings to about eight inches apart. Large, flat, fully double flowers in glowing tangerine-orange sit atop short, uniform plants ideal for edging. Remove faded heads regularly to sustain the heavy bloom through the growing season.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost once the soil warms.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep in warm soil and thin to give the tall plants room. This heirloom single-flowered French marigold shows off gold blooms boldly striped in mahogany-red like a pinwheel. Pinch early for bushiness and deadhead to extend the striped display into fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm until it sprouts, then thin to about ten inches. Fine, lacy, lemon-scented foliage forms neat mounds studded with hundreds of tiny golden single flowers. The edible, citrusy blooms are self-cleaning and pour out color from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm, sunny soil and thin the seedlings so the mounds can spread. Ferny, aromatic leaves carry a profusion of small single flowers in deep rusty-red with gold accents. The dainty edible blooms need no deadheading and keep the plants covered until first frost.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover the seed in a sunny bed and thin plants to about ten inches for full mounds. This signet blend opens masses of tiny single flowers in gold, orange, red, and bicolors over lacy, scented foliage. The edible blooms are self-cleaning, so simply enjoy the nonstop color through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Start indoors about four weeks before the last frost for early color.

directions: Start seed indoors a quarter inch deep and set out sturdy transplants after frost danger ends. This dwarf African marigold holds large, tightly double orange blooms upright on short, weather-tough stems. Deadhead as flowers fade to keep the compact plants covered in color all season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch deep in warm ground and thin to a hand's width apart. This old single-flowered French marigold bears bright golden-yellow blooms marked with a red-brown blotch at the base of each petal. Pinch young plants for bushiness and deadhead to keep the cheerful daisies coming until frost.

planting.note: Sow directly after the last frost in a sunny, open spot.

directions: Scatter seed a quarter inch deep in lean soil and thin so the tall, airy plants can sway freely. This classic blend produces large single daisies in white, pink, and rose on feathery three- to four-foot stems. Avoid rich feeding, which favors foliage over flowers, and deadhead or cut to prolong bloom until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in full sun and average soil.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in well-drained soil and thin to about a foot apart. The unusual petals of this blend roll into fluted tubes, giving shell-like flowers in white, pink, and crimson. Grow lean and stake if needed, cutting stems freely for long-lasting bouquets that keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Barely cover the seed in lean, well-drained soil and thin the seedlings to stand about a foot apart. Tall, ferny plants carry pure white single flowers that glow against the fine green foliage. Keep feeding minimal for best bloom, and cut or deadhead often to extend flowering to frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in average soil and thin to give each dwarf plant room to fill in. This compact, early strain blooms at a tidy two feet, so it needs no staking in beds or containers. White single daisies appear quickly and continue when deadheaded through the season.

planting.note: Sow after frost in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep in poor to average soil and thin to about a foot apart. This blend produces mostly double and semi-double blooms in white, rose, and cranberry on tall, graceful stems. Grow lean for the fullest flowers, and cut regularly for long-lasting bouquets that keep buds forming.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in a sunny spot.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in lean soil and thin plants to about a foot apart. Flowers open a deep ruby-red and gradually age to rosy antique shades, giving multiple tones on the plant at once. Keep soil on the poor side and deadhead to encourage waves of the richly colored blooms.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early.

directions: Barely cover the seed in average, well-drained soil and thin to give the bushy plants room. This breakthrough compact variety blooms in a soft primrose-to-pale-yellow, unusual for garden cosmos. It stays low enough for containers and borders and reblooms well with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in lean soil and thin plants to about a foot apart. Delicate single flowers blend soft pale yellow with a rosy apricot flush and darker rose eye. Grow it lean and cut often for airy bouquets, which keeps fresh buds opening into fall.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed in warm, well-drained soil and thin the compact plants to about a foot apart. This award-winning sulphur cosmos bears bright semi-double orange flowers over ferny foliage on dwarf plants. It thrives on heat and neglect, blooming quickly and steadily when deadheaded through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost danger passes.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm, lean soil and thin the seedlings to about a foot apart. This blend of sulphur cosmos opens single and semi-double flowers in vivid gold, orange, and red-orange. Extremely heat- and drought-tolerant, it blooms fast and keeps going all summer with occasional deadheading.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seeds one to two inches deep where they will grow and thin to at least eighteen inches apart. These giants soar well over head height on thick stalks topped by a single enormous seed-filled head. Provide steady water and support in windy sites, then harvest the head once the back turns brown to dry for seeds.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a sunny spot and thin plants to about a foot apart. This branching type produces many deep mahogany-red blooms with near-black centers on strong stems. Nearly pollenless, it makes a clean cut flower, and picking keeps fresh buds opening for weeks.

planting.note: Set out established plants once nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Plant this large-flowered salmon-and-coral cultivar in rich, freely draining soil with the crown level with the surrounding grade. Feed lightly every few weeks through the warm season and keep evenly moist without waterlogging. Expect the huge overlapping blooms across summer and fall; in frost-prone areas grow it in a large pot and shelter it indoors for winter.

planting.note: Move outdoors after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Grown as much for its cream-and-green marbled foliage as for its single red flowers, this variegated hibiscus wants bright light to keep the leaf coloring crisp. Plant in well-drained soil and water steadily during heat, easing off in cooler months. Pinch stem tips in spring to build a bushy, well-branched plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger has fully passed.

directions: A vigorous old favorite bearing very large single rose-pink flowers, this cultivar grows into a tall, upright shrub in frost-free gardens. Give it deep, fertile soil and consistent moisture, feeding through the growing season for continuous bloom. Prune hard in early spring to control size and encourage fresh flowering wood.

planting.note: Set out after nights warm in late spring.

directions: This heritage single-flowered hibiscus opens soft white to blush blooms with a pink flush, held on a strong, tall-growing frame. Plant in well-drained ground in full sun and keep it watered through dry spells for the best flower show. Shape with a spring prune, as it flowers readily on new growth.

planting.note: Best planted in warm spring soil once frost has ended.

directions: This classic exhibition hibiscus produces large single blooms in warm orange tones with a deeper eye. Provide rich, well-drained soil, steady moisture, and regular light feeding to support the big flowers. It performs best with warm nights, so container culture and winter shelter suit cooler climates.

planting.note: Plant out after all danger of frost.

directions: A well-known large-flowered cultivar with peach-pink petals shading to a rosy center. Plant in fertile, freely draining soil in full sun and keep evenly moist during the heat of summer. Feed regularly with a balanced fertilizer and pinch young shoots to encourage a compact, floriferous bush.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frosts have finished.

directions: This popular cultivar bears soft pink single flowers with a paler edge and a rosy center, reminiscent of apple blossom. Plant in full sun and freely draining soil, keeping it evenly watered through warm weather. Regular light feeding and a spring trim keep it blooming freely on fresh growth.

planting.note: Set out after the last spring frost.

directions: A robust old cultivar with clear single red flowers on a tall, vigorous shrub. Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun and keep it well watered while it is actively growing. Prune in early spring to shape the plant and stimulate abundant new flowering stems.

planting.note: Plant when soil is warm after frost passes.

directions: A dependable yellow-flowered cultivar with large single blooms and a small red or white eye. Grow in full sun in rich, freely draining soil and keep the roots evenly moist during hot weather. Feed through the season and pinch growing tips early on to build a full, bushy plant.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather well after the last frost.

directions: This distinctive species dangles pendant red flowers with deeply cut, recurved petals and a long protruding staminal column, resembling a hanging lantern. Give it full sun to light shade, warm conditions, and steady moisture in fertile, well-drained soil. Its arching stems look best cascading over a wall or trained as a loose informal shrub.

planting.note: Plant only in frost-free warmth in late spring.

directions: One of the few fragrant hibiscus, this Hawaiian native carries white flowers with pink-tinged stamens on a large shrub or small tree. Plant in a sheltered, frost-free spot with rich, moist, well-drained soil and full sun to part shade. Prune after a flush of bloom to keep the tall, open framework in bounds.

planting.note: Grow only where frost never occurs.

directions: This fragrant Kaua'i native opens white flowers that age to soft pink, borne on a tall shrub or small tree with a graceful habit. Give it deep, fertile, well-drained soil, warmth, and steady moisture, siting it out of drying wind. Light pruning after flowering maintains a shapely, productive canopy.

planting.note: Set out in frost-free warmth after the last frost.

directions: Hawaii's state flower, this native bears bright yellow blooms on a sprawling shrub adapted to dry, sunny sites. Nick and pre-soak the hard seed to speed germination, then grow seedlings on in warmth. Once established it tolerates lean, fast-draining soil and appreciates a dry rest between waterings.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free gardens in late spring.

directions: A rare Kaua'i native, this hibiscus opens narrow-petaled deep red flowers on an upright shrub. Grow it in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and consistent moisture in a truly frost-free location. Occasional pruning keeps the plant dense and encourages repeated flushes of bloom.

planting.note: Only for frost-free warm climates.

directions: This Hawaiian native shrub or small tree bears vivid red to orange-red flowers loved by native honeycreepers. Plant in full sun to light shade in fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly while it establishes. Prune lightly to shape and to prompt fresh flowering wood.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in moist, sunny soil.

directions: A rare East Texas native with narrow, deeply lobed leaves and large white to cream flowers marked by a dark red center. Pre-soak seed and start warm, then grow the plants in constantly moist, humus-rich soil in full sun. It dies back to the ground in fall and returns from the crown in spring.

planting.note: Set out in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: This compact native of Texas and Mexico bears bright red flowers over heart-shaped, felted leaves and thrives in heat and drought. Start seed warm and set plants in sharply drained soil in full sun. Once established it needs little water and blooms through the hottest part of the year.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free warmth after spring frost.

directions: A slender Florida and Caribbean native carrying small, pendant, bell-shaped red flowers that barely open, prized by butterflies and hummingbirds. Start seed warm and grow in well-drained soil with light shade to full sun. It stays airy and open, blooming over a long warm season with modest watering.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: This African shrub opens large lemon-yellow flowers with a deep maroon eye that close by afternoon. Start seed warm, then grow in full sun in fertile, freely draining soil with regular moisture. It flowers over a long warm season and can be pruned to keep a rounded, bushy shape.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm, sunny ground.

directions: A tall tropical annual or short-lived perennial with maroon-flushed stems and large pink-to-red flowers bearing a dark center. Start seed warm and grow in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil, staking if the stems grow lanky. It flowers steadily through the warm months and self-sows readily in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free warmth in spring.

directions: An Australian native large shrub or small tree with white, cream, or pink flowers centered by a deep maroon throat. Nick and soak the hard seed before sowing warm, then grow plants in full sun in well-drained soil. It flowers heavily in the warm season and can be pruned to keep it shrubby.

planting.note: Grow in warm, frost-free conditions.

directions: This Australian species grows into a small tree hung with large soft-pink flowers and clothed in downy, lobed leaves. Pre-treat the hard seed, start it warm, and plant out in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil. Prune when young to establish a strong single or multi-stemmed framework.

planting.note: Plant in mild, frost-free coastal gardens.

directions: An extremely rare island native, this dense shrub bears pale greenish-yellow flowers with a maroon center that deepen to purple as they age. Grow from cuttings in full sun to light shade in well-drained soil, and shelter it from hard frost. It tolerates coastal wind well and responds to pruning with tidy, bushy growth.

planting.note: Plant after frost in moist, sunny ground.

directions: A sprawling wetland shrub with prickly stems and pale yellow flowers marked by a dark maroon center. Soak the seed, start it warm, and grow the plant in constantly moist soil in full sun near a pond or bog edge. Give it room, as the arching, bristly canes spread widely over a season.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: This South African species carries dainty, pale-pink hollyhock-like flowers over soft, lobed foliage on a loose, scrambling shrub. Start seed warm and grow in full sun in light, well-drained soil, watering moderately once established. Trim the sprawling stems in spring to encourage a tidier, freer-flowering plant.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil has warmed after frost.

directions: A quick, low annual with pale cream flowers centered by a dark maroon eye, each bloom lasting only a few hours before closing. Sow seed shallowly in place after frost in full sun and average soil. It flowers within a couple of months and self-sows freely, so remove spent pods if you wish to limit spread.

planting.note: Set out after the last spring frost once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting so the crown sits level with the soil in fertile, moisture-retentive ground with afternoon shade. Pinch the growing tips two or three times in spring to build a dense, well-branched bush. Feed every couple of weeks through summer and keep the roots evenly moist for a long run of deep red and mauve bells.

planting.note: Plant in spring after hard frost has passed so roots establish before flowering.

directions: Site this vigorous hardy fuchsia in humus-rich soil with the base a couple of inches below grade so the crown is protected in winter. In colder zones leave the old stems and a mulch over the roots until spring, then cut back to live buds. Expect carmine sepals over rosy-purple skirts from midsummer to the first frosts.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot where the lax stems can spread or cascade.

directions: Give this spreading old cultivar bronze-tinted foliage room to trail over a wall or bank in well-drained, fertile soil. Water freely in dry spells and feed monthly through the growing season. The long-tubed scarlet-and-violet flowers appear over dark leaves from early summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant against a warm wall or support after frost so the tall stems can be trained upward.

directions: Train the long, upright canes of this scrambling fuchsia onto a trellis or obelisk, tying in new growth as it extends. Plant in rich, moisture-retentive soil and feed generously to fuel the vigorous climb. Small black-red and deep purple flowers cover the plant from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a container or the front of a bed where its tiny flowers can be admired.

directions: This encliandra-type fuchsia carries myriad tiny crimson flowers on fine, ferny stems, so plant it where the small scale is visible. Grow in a gritty, free-draining mix and clip lightly to keep the twiggy habit neat. It responds well to shaping and can be trained as a small standard or bonsai.

planting.note: Plant after frost where the cream-and-pink variegated leaves get good light.

directions: Grown mainly for its cream, green and pink-flushed foliage, this cultivar needs bright light to keep the variegation vivid. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and pinch out shoots to encourage bushy, colorful growth. Modest red-and-purple flowers are a bonus over the striking leaves through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a bright but sheltered spot to keep the pale foliage from scorching.

directions: This hardy form has soft grey-green leaves edged in creamy white and needs light shade to avoid leaf burn. Plant with the crown slightly buried in fertile, moist soil and mulch the roots for winter. Very pale pink-flushed flowers hang delicately along the stems from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a rock garden, trough or basket where the wiry stems can creep.

directions: This unusual New Zealand species hugs the ground with thin trailing stems and small rounded leaves. Grow it in gritty, free-draining soil and it will thread through rockwork or spill from a container. Tiny upward-facing tubeless flowers with blue pollen are followed by surprisingly large plum-red berries.

planting.note: Plant after frost; its strong growth makes an excellent standard or large bush.

directions: A robust, fast-growing cultivar that quickly forms a substantial bush and trains readily into a standard. Plant in rich soil, stake early if growing upright, and feed heavily to support the vigorous stems. Large single flowers in pink-white and vivid currant-red appear freely all summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a bed or large pot with light overhead shade.

directions: This free-flowering double bears pink sepals over a full mauve-pink skirt on an upright, bushy plant. Plant in moisture-retentive soil and pinch the shoots to build a shapely framework. Keep it fed and evenly watered for an unbroken display of plump double blooms through the season.

planting.note: Plant after frost into hanging baskets or window boxes for cascading color.

directions: One of the best basket fuchsias, sending long lax stems that drip with large rose-pink single flowers. Plant several to a basket in a peat-free multipurpose mix and pinch early for fullness. Water daily in warm weather and deadhead spent blooms to keep the cascade coming.

planting.note: Plant after frost in baskets or raised containers where the blooms hang free.

directions: This trailer produces fully double blooms in soft candy pink over glossy foliage. Set plants in a rich basket mix, pinch the tips twice, and give a high-potash feed once flowering starts. Shelter from strong midday sun keeps the delicate pink from fading.

planting.note: Plant after frost in baskets where the long slim flowers can dangle.

directions: Named for its long, narrow crimson flowers with slender recurved sepals, this vigorous trailer excels in a basket. Plant in a fertile mix and let the stems cascade well over the rim. Keep the roots moist and feed regularly for wave after wave of the spidery deep-red blooms.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered container or bed with light shade.

directions: This large-flowered double carries pale salmon sepals over a full skirt of soft dusky-pink petals. Plant in rich, moist soil and support the branches, as the heavy blooms can weigh down soft growth. Deadhead often and feed with high potash to sustain the big, showy flowers.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a border where its neat upright habit shows well.

directions: A classic exhibition single with rose sepals over a pale amethyst skirt on a tidy, self-branching bush. Plant in fertile soil and pinch two or three times for a rounded, floriferous plant. It flowers profusely and holds its shape with little staking.

planting.note: Plant after frost for a summer bedding or container display.

directions: This upright cultivar holds its flowers outward and slightly erect, so the white-and-violet blooms face the viewer. Plant in a bright, sheltered spot in well-drained soil and pinch for bushiness. The stiff, self-supporting growth makes it ideal for summer bedding schemes.

planting.note: Plant after frost in pots or the front of a border.

directions: A compact, exceptionally free-flowering single with red sepals and a crisp white skirt held out from the plant. Plant in fertile soil, pinch early, and it will smother itself in small upward-looking blooms. Its neat size suits containers and small-scale bedding.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a bush or standard in a lightly shaded spot.

directions: A soft, self-colored single in clear rose-pink with gently flared sepals on an easy, bushy plant. Plant in moist, fertile soil and pinch for a full head of bloom. Its uniform pink tones and reliable growth make it a favorite for training as a standard.

planting.note: Plant after frost with some shade to protect the pure-white blooms.

directions: An all-white single of great purity, with recurving sepals and a bell-shaped corolla that can flush faintly pink in strong light. Plant in fertile, moist soil in a spot shaded from harsh sun, which marks the delicate petals. Pinch to build an upright bush and keep evenly watered.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a border or pot with light shade.

directions: This graceful single shows pink-veined white sepals over a soft white skirt on a strong, self-branching bush. Plant in humus-rich soil and pinch a couple of times to shape it. It is a dependable, weather-tolerant grower that flowers freely for exhibition or the garden.

planting.note: Plant after frost in containers or the front edge of a bed.

directions: A dainty small-flowered single with white sepals tipped green and a delicate pink skirt, carried in abundance. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and pinch for a compact, floriferous mound. Its modest size and prolific bloom suit small pots and the front of borders.

planting.note: Plant in spring; it is borderline hardy in mild gardens with winter mulch.

directions: A vigorous semi-double with pinkish-white sepals over a purple skirt splashed with pink, on lax, spreading stems. Plant in fertile soil where it can be grown as a lax bush or trained in a basket. In milder areas mulch the roots and it may overwinter to flower again.

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger; triphyllas resent cold and need warmth to thrive.

directions: This triphylla-type bears clusters of long, slender salmon-pink tubular flowers at the shoot tips over olive-green, red-backed leaves. Plant in warm, fertile soil and keep it sheltered from cold winds. It flowers continuously and is best lifted or taken as cuttings before frost since it is not winter hardy.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sheltered position or large pot.

directions: Regarded as one of the finest triphyllas, 'Mary' carries terminal trusses of brilliant scarlet tubular flowers over dark sage-green, crimson-veined foliage. Plant in rich, warm soil and protect from any chill, which browns the leaves. Overwinter frost-free and take cuttings, as it will not survive a hard freeze.

planting.note: Plant after frost; keep warm and sheltered for best growth.

directions: An old triphylla hybrid with soft salmon-pink long-tubed flowers held in terminal clusters above downy, sage-green foliage flushed pink beneath. Plant in warm, fertile, well-drained soil and shield it from cold. Feed regularly through summer and overwinter frost-free, as the plant is fully tender.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a large tub or, in frost-free climates, as a specimen shrub.

directions: This large Central American species forms a small tree bearing airy panicles of tiny lilac-pink flowers, quite unlike typical pendent fuchsias. Plant in rich, well-drained soil and give it room and warmth to develop its stately habit. The blooms carry a light fragrance and are followed by small dark berries; protect from any frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm border or large container.

directions: A tuberous-rooted Mexican species with large soft green leaves and drooping clusters of very long, slender orange-red tubular flowers. Plant in rich, free-draining soil and keep it warm and sheltered from wind. It rests over winter, so reduce watering when growth slows and keep the roots frost-free.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sheltered spot or roomy pot.

directions: This Central American species carries curious flattened flowers with a swollen rose-red tube tipped green, said to resemble a small chili. Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in a warm position with light shade. The edible-tasting flowers and berries are a novelty; overwinter the plant frost-free.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a container or bed with light shade.

directions: A dramatic large double with red sepals over a very full, ruffled skirt of dark purple-violet. Plant in rich soil and support the stems, since the heavy flowers can bow soft growth. Deadhead and feed with high potash to keep the big blooms coming through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost in baskets or containers where blooms are seen up close.

directions: This popular double pairs crisp white sepals with a rich, deep purple-blue skirt for strong contrast. Plant in a fertile basket or pot mix and pinch for a bushy, semi-trailing shape. Regular deadheading and feeding keep the bold two-tone flowers in full production.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a container or border in light shade.

directions: A full double with red sepals over a rounded skirt that opens violet-blue and softens with age. Plant in moist, fertile soil and pinch to build a self-supporting bush. Keep well fed and watered for a steady succession of plump, colorful blooms.

planting.note: Plant after frost as a bush or standard in light shade.

directions: A vigorous, upright double with carmine sepals striped pale on the underside over a rich mauve-purple skirt. Plant in fertile soil and pinch for a strong branching framework that trains well as a standard. Feed and water generously for large, freely produced double flowers.

planting.note: Plant after frost in pots or bedding in a lightly shaded spot.

directions: This compact single holds pale pink-white sepals and a soft mauve-pink skirt outward and upright above the foliage. Plant in fertile soil and pinch for a neat, densely flowered bush. Its self-branching habit and abundant bloom make it excellent for small pots and edging.

planting.note: Plant after frost in baskets or containers where the lax stems can spill.

directions: A free-flowering semi-trailer with white sepals over a mauve-pink skirt, good for baskets and low containers. Plant several to a basket in a rich mix and pinch the shoots to fill out the display. Water daily in heat and deadhead to keep the blooms flowing all season.

planting.note: Plant after frost with shade from strong sun to protect the white blooms.

directions: A choice double in near-white, the sepals and full skirt washed faintly pink toward the base. Plant in fertile, moist soil in a position sheltered from hot sun, which discolors the pale petals. Pinch to shape and feed regularly for a graceful, softly colored display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile soil; it overwinters outdoors in mild zones.

directions: A hardy upright with carmine sepals over a rosy-mauve skirt, carried on stems with slightly yellow-green foliage. Plant with the crown set a little below soil level and mulch the roots for winter protection. Cut back to live wood in spring, and it will bloom reliably from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost as a bush or standard in a lightly shaded position.

directions: A neat, self-branching single with rose-pink sepals over a pale pink skirt, held nicely on a strong upright plant. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and pinch two or three times for a shapely bush. It flowers freely and trains well into a compact standard.

planting.note: Set out sprawling plants once nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Root softwood cuttings in gritty, fast-draining mix and pot on once new growth appears. Give a warm, sheltered spot with lean soil, as rich feeding softens the aromatic foliage. Pinch the trailing stems to keep the plant bushy and enjoy the rose scent whenever the leaves are brushed.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into sharply drained soil.

directions: Propagate from cuttings or divisions of its tuberous roots and grow in a gritty, well-drained mix. Keep it on the dry side and let it rest through winter to trigger the best flowering. Magenta blooms rise on wiry stems above the soft grey-green kidney-shaped leaves through the warm months.

planting.note: Move outdoors once the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Take stem cuttings in spring and root them in a lean, quick-draining medium. The fleshy blue-green leaves have a tart, sorrel-like flavor and store water, so water sparingly and never let the roots stay wet. Slender salmon-pink flowers appear over a long season on lightly succulent stems.

planting.note: Best grown in a pot that can be moved under cover in cold weather.

directions: Root cuttings in gritty compost and grow this low shrublet slightly pot-bound for best flowering. The pansy-like blooms are red and white with a dark eye, unusual among pelargoniums. Water lightly, feed rarely, and protect from winter wet to keep the plant healthy.

planting.note: Grow in a container of cactus-type mix and shelter from frost.

directions: Let cuttings callus a day or two before rooting them in a sharply drained succulent mix. The angular, nearly leafless green stems store water, so water only when the mix is fully dry and keep the plant dry in winter. Large pale pink two-petaled flowers appear on the sprawling stems in spring and summer.

planting.note: Keep in a pot so it can rest dry and frost-free in winter.

directions: Grow from cuttings in a gritty, free-draining mix and water only while the plant is in active growth. The swollen, knuckle-like nodes give the plant its name and store moisture through its summer dormancy. Clusters of small greenish-yellow flowers release a sweet fragrance in the evening.

planting.note: Give this large shrubby species room and shelter from frost.

directions: Root cuttings in spring and grow in fertile, well-drained soil where the plant has space to reach shrub size. The large soft leaves have a pungent scent when touched, and the flowers resemble tiny pink and maroon butterflies. Cut back hard after flowering to keep the plant compact and encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a warm, well-drained bed or pot.

directions: Strike cuttings in a gritty mix and grow this compact shrub on the dry, lean side. The small toothed leaves resemble birch foliage and carry a balsam-like scent when crushed. Showy pink flowers with darker veining appear over a long spring-to-summer season.

planting.note: Set out after frost with room to develop into a small shrub.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and grow in well-drained soil in a sunny, sheltered position. The heart-shaped leaves are green above and felted white beneath, and the plant carries pink flowers with darker markings for many weeks. Prune after the main flush to maintain a tidy, bushy shape.

planting.note: Grow in a container of free-draining mix moved under cover in winter.

directions: Root cuttings easily in gritty compost and grow this dwarf, spreading species slightly dry. The small deeply cut leaves smell of celery when handled, and bright magenta flowers appear almost year-round in mild conditions. Trim lightly to keep the low mound neat and floriferous.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in lean, sharply drained soil.

directions: Grow from cuttings in a gritty mix and keep the plant on the dry side for compact growth. The finely divided grey-green leaves are aromatic like southernwood when brushed. Small white to pink flowers with red markings dot the plant through the warm season.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, well-drained spot or pot.

directions: Root cuttings in a fast-draining mix and grow in full sun with sparing water. The finely dissected, sticky leaves carry a resinous pine fragrance when touched. Small lilac-pink flowers appear over summer above the ferny, aromatic foliage.

planting.note: Plant out after frost with room for shrubby growth.

directions: Take cuttings in spring and grow in well-drained soil in a sunny position. The deeply cut leaves are rough to the touch and give off a rose-lemon scent, making this a favorite for potpourri. Prune after flowering to keep the upright shrub dense and shapely.

planting.note: Grow in a pot of gritty mix and rest it dry after flowering.

directions: Root cuttings in a sharply drained medium and grow this semi-succulent species on the lean, dry side. Brilliant scarlet flowers are held above softly silvery, lobed leaves in spring. Allow a dry summer rest, as the plant naturally slows growth in the hottest months.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in well-drained soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Grow from fresh seed or cuttings in a free-draining mix and keep on the dry side. The rounded, lobed leaves often carry a darker horseshoe zone, and small cream-to-pink flowers appear over a long period. This spreading, mat-forming species tolerates poor soils and mild neglect.

planting.note: Grow in a pot of gritty mix, sheltered from frost.

directions: Root cuttings in a lean, well-drained mix and grow the plant slightly dry. The small grey-green leaves smell strongly of balm or pennyroyal when brushed. Pale pink to lilac flowers with darker veins are produced over a long season on this compact shrublet.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sharply drained soil.

directions: Grow from cuttings in a gritty mix and keep this low, spreading species on the dry side. The small, finely divided leaves give a subtle scent when handled. Delicate white to pale pink flowers with fine red markings are borne over a long spring-to-summer season.

planting.note: Grow in a pot of gritty mix and rest dry after flowering.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and grow this hybrid in a sharply drained, lean mix. Small deeply cut greyish leaves arise from a partly tuberous base, so keep it dry during its summer rest. Sprays of vivid scarlet flowers with dark centers glow above the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant out around the last frost in well-drained soil.

directions: Sow scarified seed in warmth or root cuttings in a free-draining mix. This tough, spreading species from Australia tolerates coastal conditions and poor soils with sparing water. Clusters of pale pink flowers with darker veins are held above softly hairy, lobed leaves through summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost with room for a large shrub.

directions: Root cuttings in spring and grow in well-drained soil where the shrub has room to expand. The large lobed leaves have a strong balsamic, rose-like scent when touched. Deep pink flowers with dark feathering appear over a long season; cut back after flowering to keep the plant compact.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after all danger of frost.

directions: Root stem cuttings in spring and grow in free-draining potting mix. The tidy rounded leaves carry a fine dark ring near the margin, giving this old cultivar its neat look. Deadhead the small scarlet flower clusters to keep bloom coming through summer.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sun to light afternoon shade.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and grow in bright light for the best leaf color. Each green leaf shows a bold cream-yellow butterfly splash at the center, making the foliage the main show. Red flowers add contrast; pinch stems to keep the plant bushy and remove spent blooms.

planting.note: Set out after frost where leaf variegation can show.

directions: Root cuttings and grow in well-drained mix with steady but not heavy feeding. The green leaves are broadly edged in creamy white and topped with bright red flower heads. Give good light for crisp variegation and pinch to encourage a full, branching habit.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: Grow this dwarf fancy-leaf type from cuttings in a bright position. Golden leaves carry a bold bronze-red zone, and the plant is crowned with double orange-red flowers. Keep it in strong light and lean soil so the leaf coloring stays vivid.

planting.note: Set out after frost in full sun for the best flowering.

directions: Root cuttings in spring and grow in free-draining mix with regular deadheading. Each red floret is edged in white, giving the flower heads a striking bicolor look that can vary from bloom to bloom. Water evenly and feed lightly for a long, showy display.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in beds or large containers.

directions: Set rooted plants out after frost in fertile, well-drained soil or potting mix. This vigorous vegetative series produces large flower heads on strong stems and stands up well to summer heat. Deadhead regularly and feed every couple of weeks for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about 10 weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow seed indoors in warmth and barely cover, keeping the mix evenly moist until seedlings appear. Grow on in bright light and transplant after frost into beds or containers. This uniform seed series has clearly zoned leaves and rounded flower heads in many colors.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in containers or well-drained beds.

directions: Set out rooted cuttings after frost in fertile, free-draining mix. This compact vegetative series holds tidy, rounded flower heads well above the foliage and stays neat in pots. Deadhead and feed regularly for a steady show through the warm months.

planting.note: Set out after frost in full sun for heavy flowering.

directions: Grow rooted plants in free-draining mix and set out once nights are warm. This vigorous vegetative series is known for weather tolerance and large, brightly colored flower heads. Remove spent trusses and feed lightly to keep the bloom coming all season.

planting.note: Grow in small pots and set out after frost in bright light.

directions: Root cuttings and grow this true dwarf in small pots of gritty mix. The nearly black-green leaves set off bright scarlet flowers on a very compact plant. Give it strong light and avoid overwatering to keep the dark foliage and tight habit.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in baskets or containers to trail.

directions: Root cuttings and grow this trailing ivy type in baskets or window boxes. The cream-edged leaves flush pink or purple when the plant is kept slightly dry and cool. White flowers add a soft touch; pinch tips to encourage branching along the cascading stems.

planting.note: Set out after frost in containers or hanging baskets.

directions: Grow from cuttings and let the trailing stems spill from baskets or pots. The glossy leaves are netted with striking cream veins that give the plant its name. Pink flowers appear over the patterned foliage; provide bright light but shelter from harsh midday sun.

planting.note: Plant out after frost to cascade from baskets and boxes.

directions: Root cuttings and train the trailing stems over the edges of containers. The double pink flowers are tightly cupped like little rosebuds, opening only partway. Deadhead spent clusters and keep evenly moist for a long, generous display.

planting.note: Set out after frost in baskets, boxes, or as ground trailer.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and grow this classic ivy type for its vigorous cascade. Glossy ivy-shaped leaves clothe long stems topped with rounded heads of double rose-pink flowers. Feed lightly and remove old blooms to keep the plant flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Start seed indoors 10 to 12 weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow seed early indoors in warmth, as this trailing ivy type is slow from seed. Grow seedlings in bright light and move to baskets after frost. Long stems cascade with flowers in a mix of pinks, reds, lavender, and white through the season.

planting.note: Set out after frost in containers and hanging baskets.

directions: Root cuttings and let this trailing ivy spill from pots and baskets. The semi-double flowers are white brushed and edged with cherry-red, giving a candy-striped effect. Deadhead often and keep evenly moist to prolong the striking bicolor display.

planting.note: Grow in pots and place out after frost in bright, cool conditions.

directions: Root cuttings in late summer and grow in free-draining mix in a bright, airy spot. This classic regal bears large ruffled flowers of deep wine-black edged with a lighter rim. Regals flower best after cool nights, so give them a spring flush and trim back after blooming.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a bright but not scorching position.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and grow in a cool, bright spot for the largest flowers. The big frilled blooms are strawberry-red heavily marked and feathered with white. Feed lightly, water evenly, and cut back after the main flush to shape the plant for next year.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in containers or protected beds.

directions: Grow rooted plants of this regal series in free-draining mix in bright light. Large ruffled flower heads come in a wide color range and are showy in spring and early summer. Deadhead spent trusses and trim after flowering to keep plants compact and healthy.

planting.note: Grow in pots and place outside after frost in a bright spot.

directions: Root cuttings and grow this compact angel type in small pots of free-draining mix. It carries masses of small pansy-faced flowers in mauve-purple with darker upper petals. Angels bloom over a long season, so deadhead lightly and pinch to keep the plant bushy.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, well-drained spot.

directions: Root cuttings and grow this upright scented type in free-draining mix on the lean side. The small crinkled leaves release a sharp lemon scent when brushed and can be used for flavoring. Small lilac-pink flowers appear in summer; clip regularly to keep the columnar plant dense.

planting.note: Plant out after frost with room for a vigorous plant.

directions: Grow from cuttings in well-drained mix; this scented hybrid is more vigorous than most. The lobed leaves have a spicy cedar-rose fragrance when handled. Unusually for a scented type, it bears large, showy rose-pink flowers with darker markings through summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, well-drained bed or pot.

directions: Root cuttings and grow this oak-leaf scented type in free-draining mix. The deeply lobed, dark-centered leaves smell of balsam and spice when touched. Small mauve-pink flowers appear in summer; pinch stems to keep the shrubby plant full and compact.

planting.note: Plant out after frost with room to reach a good size.

directions: Grow from cuttings in well-drained soil; this vigorous rose-scented type gets large. The irregularly cut grey-green leaves have a blended lemon and rose fragrance prized for cooking and potpourri. Pinch often to shape the plant and enjoy small pink flowers in summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Root cuttings and grow this bushy scented type in free-draining mix. The lightly lobed green leaves give off a warm ginger fragrance when brushed. Bright pink flowers appear over a long summer season; pinch the tips to encourage a spreading, well-branched plant.

planting.note: Move outdoors only after nights stay reliably above 55 F.

directions: Root a leaf or stem cutting in a loose, peaty mix and pot up once anchored. Prize the silver-and-green spiraled leaves in bright indirect light, and let the surface dry slightly between waterings to avoid rot. Small pink blooms appear on wiry stems in spring but the foliage is the main show.

planting.note: Best kept as a warm, humid houseplant except in frost-free zones.

directions: Start from a leaf-vein or rhizome cutting in a shallow, well-drained tray. Grow in bright filtered light to bring out the purple, pink, and silver banding, keeping humidity high and roots evenly moist but never soggy. Pinch off flower stalks if you want the plant to funnel energy into leaves.

planting.note: Site in warm shade outdoors only through the frost-free months.

directions: Propagate from a rooted leaf cutting and pot into a chunky, moisture-retentive mix. The red, silver, and green rings color best in bright indirect light with steady warmth and humidity. Water when the top inch dries and avoid wetting the foliage to keep mildew away.

planting.note: Give warmth and shelter; it resents cold drafts and frost.

directions: Root cuttings in a humid, well-aerated medium and shift up gradually as growth resumes. The deeply cut, maple-shaped silver leaves with dark veins want bright indirect light and consistent moisture. Feed lightly through the growing season and cut back leggy growth to keep it full.

planting.note: Keep frost-free; thrives in a terrarium or shaded patio in summer.

directions: Divide the creeping rhizome or root a leaf cutting into a shallow pot of open mix. The spiraled bronze leaves edged in red hairs love high humidity and bright, indirect light. Keep evenly moist and pinch spent flower stems to show off the foliage.

planting.note: Move outside after frost and bring back in before nights turn cold.

directions: Root stem cuttings with a node in water or moist mix, then pot into rich, free-draining soil. Stake the tall bamboo-like canes and give bright light for the heaviest clusters of pink flowers. Cut older canes back hard in spring to force fresh basal growth and repeat blooming.

planting.note: Grows as a large frost-free container or greenhouse plant.

directions: Take a cane cutting below a node and root it in a warm, humid spot before potting on. The silver-spotted wings and pendulous pink flower trusses show best in bright, filtered light. Support the canes, keep soil lightly moist, and prune tall stems in spring to keep the plant bushy.

planting.note: A tender cane begonia for warm patios and frost-free borders.

directions: Propagate from nodal stem cuttings rooted in a warm, humid environment. Bronze-green leaves and drooping coral-pink flower clusters develop best in bright indirect light with steady feeding. Cane begonias flower nearly year-round in warmth, so remove faded trusses and prune leggy stems to encourage new canes.

planting.note: Keep above freezing; excels as a shaded summer container plant.

directions: Root a cane cutting in moist mix and pot into rich, well-drained soil once established. The metallic silver leaves with green veins and burgundy backs need bright indirect light to hold their sheen. Stake the stems, water when the surface dries, and cut back in spring for renewed clusters of pale pink bloom.

planting.note: A vigorous old cane cultivar for large frost-free pots.

directions: Start from stem cuttings taken below a node and root in a warm, humid spot. Silver-flecked olive leaves and pendant coral-red flowers appear on tall canes given bright filtered light. Provide support, keep soil evenly moist during growth, and prune old canes in spring to keep it productive.

planting.note: New Guinea species that must stay frost-free and humid.

directions: Root soft stem cuttings in a warm, humid propagator and pot into an airy, moisture-retentive mix. The jagged bronze leaves flecked with raised pink spots color best in bright indirect light. Keep humidity high, water when the top layer dries, and pinch tips to build a fuller, well-branched plant.

planting.note: A tough, near-everblooming shrub begonia for shaded frost-free beds.

directions: Root easy stem cuttings in moist mix and set out after frost in dappled shade. Glossy dark leaves with red undersides back near-continuous sprays of pink flowers. It tolerates more sun and neglect than most begonias; shear lightly to keep it dense and blooming.

planting.note: Popular maple-leaf begonia grown frost-free indoors or on shaded patios.

directions: Divide the surface rhizome or root a leaf cutting into a shallow, wide pot of open mix. The star-shaped chartreuse-and-bronze leaves color richly in bright indirect light. Let the top dry between waterings, and enjoy the tall stalks of pale pink winter flowers held above the foliage.

planting.note: An old, forgiving houseplant begonia kept above freezing.

directions: Divide the thick creeping rhizome or root a leaf into a shallow pot of loose mix. Round, glossy leaves that are olive above and red beneath give the plant its name and take bright indirect light well. Water when the surface dries, and airy pink flower sprays rise on tall stems in late winter.

planting.note: Large-leaved rhizomatous begonia for warm, shaded containers.

directions: Root a wedge of rhizome or a leaf cutting in a shallow, wide pot of chunky mix. The big chartreuse-gold leaves splashed with chocolate brown want bright, indirect light to keep their contrast. Keep humidity up, let the surface dry slightly between waterings, and pale flowers appear on tall stalks in winter.

planting.note: A small mounding begonia ideal for terrariums and windowsills.

directions: Divide the compact rhizome or root a leaf into a shallow pot of fast-draining mix. The small bronze leaves with lime-green markings mound densely in bright indirect light. Keep humidity moderate to high, water when the top dries, and slender pink flowers appear above the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Ruffled rhizomatous begonia grown frost-free for its foliage.

directions: Root a rhizome piece or leaf cutting in a shallow, wide container of loose mix. The large, ruffled apple-green leaves with red-flushed veins fill out in bright indirect light. Water when the surface dries, keep humidity up, and enjoy the tall pale pink flower stalks in late winter.

planting.note: Star-leaved rhizomatous begonia for warm, shaded spots.

directions: Divide the rhizome or root a leaf cutting into a shallow pot of open, well-drained mix. The deeply lobed star-shaped leaves are green with dark bronze centers and show best in bright filtered light. Let the surface dry between waterings and expect airy pink flowers on tall stems in winter.

planting.note: Bold-leaved hybrid grown frost-free in shade or as a houseplant.

directions: Divide the stout rhizome or root a leaf into a wide, shallow pot of chunky mix. Large bronze-green star-shaped leaves on red hairy stalks make a dramatic clump in bright indirect light. Water when the surface dries, and tall stems of pale pink flowers rise well above the foliage in late winter.

planting.note: Mexican species grown frost-free for foliage and winter bloom.

directions: Root a rhizome section or leaf in a shallow pot of loose, well-drained mix. The large, glossy pale-green leaves with red-fringed stalk collars want bright indirect light. Let the surface dry between waterings, and tall airy sprays of pink flowers appear in late winter.

planting.note: Ruffled-leaf rhizomatous begonia for warm, shaded conditions.

directions: Divide the rhizome or root a leaf cutting into a shallow, wide pot of airy mix. Round, ruffled lime-green leaves banded with chocolate brown around the edge want bright indirect light. Keep humidity up, water when the top dries, and pale flowers rise on slender stems in winter.

planting.note: Textured rhizomatous begonia grown frost-free for foliage.

directions: Root a rhizome division or leaf in a shallow pot of chunky, fast-draining mix. The puckered golden-bronze leaves with dark markings deepen in color under bright indirect light. Let the surface dry between waterings, keep humidity moderate, and pale pink flowers appear on tall stems in winter.

planting.note: Brazilian species whose leaves shift color with light; keep frost-free.

directions: Root a rhizome piece or leaf in a shallow pot of moisture-retentive mix. The puckered leaves turn from bright green to deep bronze depending on light intensity, so adjust brightness to taste. Keep humidity high, water when the surface dries, and small white flowers appear on short stems.

planting.note: Succulent-stemmed Brazilian species needing warmth and good drainage.

directions: Root a thick stem cutting in a gritty, fast-draining mix and pot into similar soil. The kidney-shaped leaves are coated in dense white felt, so water at the base and keep foliage dry to prevent rot. Give bright light, water sparingly, and enjoy the fragrant white flowers held above the plant in summer.

planting.note: South African caudex species; keep frost-free and give a winter rest.

directions: Grow from a stem cutting or surface-sown dust-fine seed pressed onto moist mix and kept warm. This caudex-forming species develops a swollen woody base and small maple-shaped leaves flecked with silver. Water freely in growth, ease off as it drops leaves in winter, and small white flowers appear in the warm months.

planting.note: Trailing shrub species with fern-like foliage; keep frost-free.

directions: Root stem cuttings easily in moist mix and pot into airy, well-drained soil. The arching stems lined with tiny leaves look like a fern and cascade nicely from a hanging basket. Keep evenly moist in bright indirect light, and small white to pink flowers dangle beneath the foliage.

planting.note: Brazilian thick-leaved species grown frost-free for its foliage.

directions: Root a stem cutting in moist, well-drained mix and pot on once established. The heavy, waxy leaves are olive-green above and blood-red beneath, most striking with backlight. Give bright indirect light, water when the surface dries, and clusters of white flowers appear in late winter.

planting.note: Thick-rhizomed Mexican species with palmate leaves; keep frost-free.

directions: Divide the stout creeping rhizome or root a section in an open, well-drained mix. Large hand-shaped leaves radiate from a thick, upright rhizome that resembles a small trunk. Keep in bright indirect light, water when the surface dries, and pink-tinged flower clusters emerge in late winter.

planting.note: Brazilian species with velvety leaves; keep warm and frost-free.

directions: Root a rhizome piece or leaf into a shallow pot of loose, airy mix. The rounded, velvety green leaves have striking rich red undersides that glow when backlit. Keep humidity up, water at the base when the surface dries, and pale flowers appear on short stems.

planting.note: Sumatran species needing high humidity and steady warmth.

directions: Root a rhizome section or leaf in a shallow, humid propagator with an airy mix. The peltate, quilted leaves are bronze-green with a metallic sheen and dark veining. This species demands high humidity, so grow it in a terrarium or humid case, keep evenly moist, and give bright indirect light.

planting.note: Brazilian species with deeply textured leaves; keep frost-free and humid.

directions: Divide the creeping rhizome or root a leaf into an open, well-drained mix. The peltate leaves are heavily puckered with a netted, blistered surface that catches the light. Keep humidity high, water when the surface dries, and white flowers rise on tall stems in the warm season.

planting.note: Trailing, fragrant-flowered species ideal for hanging baskets; keep frost-free.

directions: Root soft stem cuttings in moist mix and grow in a hanging basket to display the trailing stems. Glossy heart-shaped leaves back sweetly scented white flowers marked with a red eye. Keep evenly moist in bright indirect light, and pinch the tips to encourage branching and heavier bloom.

planting.note: Novelty species that sprouts leaflets along its veins; keep frost-free and humid.

directions: Root a stem cutting in a warm, humid mix and pot into airy, well-drained soil. This oddity grows small leaflets along the tops of its main leaf veins, giving it its name. Keep humidity high and soil evenly moist in bright indirect light, and clusters of white flowers appear in the warm months.

planting.note: Chinese species with velvety multicolored leaves; needs constant humidity.

directions: Root a leaf or rhizome piece in a humid, warm propagator with a fine, airy mix. The velvety leaves blend green, red, and silver with dense colored hairs and demand high humidity. Grow in a terrarium or humid case in bright indirect light, keep evenly moist, and enjoy the soft pink flowers.

planting.note: Vietnamese species with fringed hairy leaves; keep humid and frost-free.

directions: Divide the creeping rhizome or root a leaf in a humid, airy mix. The large green leaves are edged and topped with long red hairs, giving a striking fringed texture. Keep humidity high, water when the surface dries, and grow in bright indirect light; pale flowers rise on short stalks.

planting.note: Prized Malaysian species with netted leaves; needs constant warmth and humidity.

directions: Root a rhizome piece or leaf in a fine, humid mix within a covered case. The small puckered leaves are deep bronze-red with a bright green netted vein pattern. This choice species needs high humidity and steady warmth, so keep it in a terrarium with bright indirect light and even moisture.

planting.note: Small Central American species with cupped peltate leaves; keep frost-free.

directions: Divide the small rhizome or root a leaf into a shallow pot of well-drained mix. The rounded, shell-cupped leaves are glossy green, often with a red splash at the vein junction. Keep humidity moderate to high, water when the surface dries, and clusters of small red-tinged flowers rise in winter.

planting.note: New Guinea species with fern-cut leaves; needs high humidity and warmth.

directions: Root soft stem cuttings in a warm, humid propagator with an airy mix. Unusually for a begonia, the leaves are deeply dissected like a fern frond. This species needs consistently high humidity, so grow it in a terrarium or humid greenhouse with bright indirect light and even moisture.

planting.note: Trailing Mexican species good for baskets; keep frost-free.

directions: Root stem cuttings in moist mix and grow in a hanging basket for the trailing habit. Small dark green leaves, sometimes with a chocolate zone, back pink flowers that dangle beneath the stems. Keep evenly moist in bright indirect light and pinch tips to keep the plant full.

planting.note: Brazilian shrub-type species grown frost-free for foliage and bloom.

directions: Root a stem cutting in moist, well-drained mix and pot on once established. The pointed green leaves have pale veins and a slightly hairy texture on upright stems. Keep evenly moist in bright indirect light, feed lightly in growth, and white flowers appear in the warm season.

planting.note: Tiny creeping species ideal for terrariums; needs warmth and humidity.

directions: Root a small rhizome piece or leaf in a fine, humid mix inside a covered case. This miniature forms a dense low mat of tiny bronze-green hairy leaves, perfect for terrarium foreground. Keep humidity high and the mix lightly moist in bright indirect light, and small pink flowers dot the mat.

planting.note: Upright foliage hybrid widely used as a container thriller in shade.

directions: Grown from cuttings, this robust hybrid is prized for its bold palmate silver-and-green leaves rather than flowers. Plant in rich, well-drained mix after frost and give bright shade to part sun. Water when the top inch dries and bring indoors before frost, where it makes an easy, upright houseplant.

planting.note: South African caudex species with arrowhead leaves; keep frost-free.

directions: Root a stem cutting in a gritty, fast-draining mix to encourage the swollen woody base. The small arrowhead-shaped leaves sit on dark wiry stems above a caudex-like trunk. Water freely in growth and reduce sharply during winter dormancy, giving bright light; small white flowers appear in the warm months.

planting.note: Twisted-leaf novelty begonia grown frost-free and humid.

directions: Root a leaf or stem cutting in a humid, airy mix and pot on when established. The puckered, twisted leaves are translucent green and bronze, glowing like stained glass when backlit. Keep humidity high and the mix lightly moist in bright indirect light, and enjoy the pale flowers on short stems.

planting.note: Set out in spring once hard frosts have passed, or in early fall while roots can still establish.

directions: Plant the grafted tree with the root flare at or just above grade in rich, well-drained soil, and water deeply while it settles in. Prized for holding its deep purple-red leaves through summer heat better than most upright cultivars. Leaf out is later than 'Bloodgood', which helps it dodge late spring frosts, and autumn brings a fiery scarlet turn.

planting.note: Best planted in spring after frost or in autumn so roots knit in before summer stress.

directions: Dig a wide, shallow hole and set the tree at nursery depth in loose, humus-rich soil that drains freely. Its wine-red foliage glows almost translucent when backlit by morning sun, so site it where low light can pass through the canopy. Give it afternoon shade in hot regions to keep the color from scorching.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall into moist, well-drained ground with shelter from harsh wind.

directions: Position the root ball level with the surrounding soil and mulch lightly to hold moisture without piling against the trunk. Green through the growing season, it is grown chiefly for one of the most brilliant crimson autumn displays of any maple. Steady moisture and some direct sun bring out the deepest fall red.

planting.note: Spring or fall planting suits it; guard the bright spring foliage from strong midday sun.

directions: Plant in cool, evenly moist soil with dappled light, keeping the root zone mulched. Spring leaves emerge a glowing orange-yellow edged in red, mature to soft green-gold, and return to golden orange in fall. Too much hot sun bleaches and scorches the delicate early color, so filtered light is ideal.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered spot with afternoon shade to protect the variegation.

directions: Set the tree at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and shelter it from drying wind and hot afternoon sun. The small blue-green leaves are irregularly margined in cream and pink, giving an airy, upright silhouette well suited to narrow spaces. Prune out any all-green reverting shoots to preserve the variegated character.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; its dense form tolerates more sun than many cultivars.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in well-drained soil and expect a slow-growing, stiffly upright shrub-tree. The crinkled, tightly curled deep green leaves cluster along the branches like a lion's mane and turn gold, orange, and crimson late in fall. Its compact density makes it a favorite for containers and bonsai.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall into moist, well-drained soil with light afternoon shade.

directions: Set the tree level with the soil surface and keep the root zone cool with mulch and steady moisture. Nearly unique among the finely dissected lace-leaf maples, it grows upright rather than weeping, with feathery green leaves that redden to orange-crimson in fall. Shelter the delicate foliage from hot, drying wind.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; this laceleaf takes summer heat better than most weeping forms.

directions: Plant the cascading tree at grade and mulch to hold moisture, allowing the branches to drape into a low mound. The finely cut crimson-purple leaves hold their color through summer better than many dissectums before turning scarlet in autumn. It excels spilling over a wall or beside a pond edge.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall into rich, well-drained soil with protection from harsh afternoon sun.

directions: Set the weeping tree at nursery depth and let the strong, cascading branches build a broad mounded crown. The deeply dissected purple-red leaves are more robust and sun-tolerant than many laceleafs and blaze crimson in fall. Water regularly during dry spells to keep the fine foliage from crisping.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; give it dappled light to protect the green summer foliage.

directions: Plant at grade in moist, well-drained soil and mulch the root zone to keep it cool. The strongly weeping habit and finely cut green leaves cascade like flowing water, turning gold and crimson in autumn. Prune only to lift and shape the mound as it matures.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall into well-drained soil; a vigorous laceleaf that colors best with some sun.

directions: Set the mounding tree at nursery depth and allow it to spread into a low, weeping dome. The deeply divided leaves emerge and hold a rich garnet-red, one of the more heat-tolerant dissectums, before turning bright scarlet in fall. Give it good air movement and even moisture through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; the green laceleaf foliage prefers filtered light.

directions: Plant at grade in cool, moist, well-drained soil and mulch to protect the shallow roots. This classic green weeping laceleaf forms a graceful cascading mound and finishes the year in glowing gold, orange, and red. Site it in dappled shade to keep the fine leaves from scorching.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a compact laceleaf that keeps its red color well in sun.

directions: Plant the slow-growing weeping tree at nursery depth in well-drained soil and mulch lightly. Its finely dissected leaves hold a deep purple-red through summer better than many laceleafs, making a tidy mound ideal for containers. Fall brings a bright apple-red finish.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; protect the fine red foliage from hot afternoon sun.

directions: Set the low weeping tree at grade in moist, well-drained soil and keep the roots cool with mulch. New spring growth carries a silvery down over deep red, dissected leaves that mature to crimson-purple and turn scarlet in fall. It forms a dense, cascading mound well suited to a focal spot.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in dappled shade to keep the pale variegation from burning.

directions: Plant in cool, evenly moist soil with protection from direct midday sun, which scorches the delicate leaves. The foliage is heavily mottled with white and pink over green, giving the tree a soft, cloud-like appearance that inspired its name. Remove any plain green reverting branches to hold the variegation.
